worth_it_explanation: Invalid traffic (IVT) filtering determines whether the impressions Amazon DSP reports are real people. Amazon applies baseline IVT filtering across its inventory, but that filtering does not automatically extend to all third-party supply or to fraud segments available through Amazon's verification partnerships. A gap in filtering means spend flows to non-human activity, and the performance data that feeds campaign decisions. Click-through rates, detail page views, and attributed purchases all overstate real audience engagement when non-human traffic is counted. These checks confirm that Amazon's fraud controls are active where they apply and that the line-item configuration is not inadvertently bypassing them.
intro: Amazon DSP applies IVT filtering by default across Amazon owned-and-operated inventory. Third-party exchange supply carries more variable risk. The controls available there, including suspicious domain and app exclusions and brand safety segments from Amazon's verification partners, require active configuration at the line-item level. These checks confirm that IVT filtering is in place, that domains and apps showing anomalous performance metrics have been investigated and excluded where warranted, and that fraud exclusion segments are applied to the highest-risk third-party supply.

audit_checks.title: IVT filtering is not confirmed as active on third-party exchange supply
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Amazon's default IVT filtering covers owned-and-operated inventory but does not guarantee identical coverage across all third-party exchanges the DSP accesses. When a practitioner has never confirmed the filtering status on third-party supply, spend may be flowing to non-human activity, and the performance metrics reported for those line items overstate real audience reach.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP filters general invalid traffic (GIVT) on its owned-and-operated (O&O) inventory, which includes Amazon.com, IMDb, Twitch, Fire TV, and Prime Video. GIVT is the category of non-human traffic that is identifiable through standard means, including bot crawlers, data center traffic, and known non-human user agents identified by lists such as the IAB Tech Lab Spiders and Bots List.

Third-party exchange supply that Amazon DSP accesses through its programmatic connections is a different case. The filtering applied there depends on the exchange's own controls and on what Amazon applies at its connection point. Sophisticated invalid traffic (SIVT), which covers harder-to-detect activity such as adware, hijacked devices, and domain spoofing, is not uniformly filtered by any single party across the open exchange.

Amazon DSP's reporting does not surface a per-line-item breakdown of IVT filtered versus delivered. The place to look is anomalous performance signals on third-party supply: click-through rates that are outliers compared to O&O line items running the same creative, suspiciously high delivery volume from a small number of domains or apps, or detail page view rates that do not convert to any downstream action.

A few signs point to a gap worth investigating:
  • Third-party supply line items show click-through rates that are multiples higher than O&O line items in the same campaign
  • A small set of domains or apps accounts for a disproportionate share of impressions with low or no downstream conversion activity
  • The campaign has never had its supply reporting reviewed against Amazon's brand safety and quality controls
You may want to confirm with your Amazon DSP account team which IVT controls are active on the third-party supply sources your campaigns access.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For endemic advertisers, IVT on third-party supply dilutes the purchase and new-to-brand signals that Amazon Attribution and Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC) report. An inflated impression count from non-human traffic makes the cost-per-detail-page-view and cost-per-purchase metrics appear worse than the real audience engagement justifies, which can lead to budget shifts away from supply that is actually working.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For non-endemic advertisers driving to off-Amazon destinations, IVT on third-party supply inflates impressions without any corresponding downstream signal. The Amazon Ad Tag pixel fires on the landing page where real visitors land. A gap between impressions reported in Amazon DSP and traffic observed in site analytics is a signal that a share of reported delivery may not represent real users.

learn_more.source_title: IAB/ABC International Spiders and Bots List
A beginner's guide to brand safety

investigate.intro: Pull supply-level performance data in the Amazon DSP console to identify third-party placements with anomalous metrics, and confirm with your Amazon account team which IVT controls apply to the exchanges the account accesses.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Open the Amazon DSP console and navigate to the campaign or line item you want to review.

Apply a supply breakdown in the reporting tab to see delivery segmented by supply source or domain and app. Look for placements where impressions are high but detail page views, purchases, or off-Amazon pixel events are at or near zero.

Compare the click-through rate on third-party supply line items against O&O supply line items running the same creative in the same campaign. A rate that is materially higher on third-party supply is a signal worth investigating.
Note any domain or app that accounts for more than 15-20% of impressions in a third-party supply line item but produces a disproportionately low share of downstream conversions.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether any third-party supply placements show anomalous click-through rates, high impression concentration in a small number of domains or apps, or a pattern of impressions with no downstream conversion activity. Line items with no third-party supply or with no anomalous signals pass. Line items where anomalous placements have not been reviewed or excluded are recorded as a finding with the placement name and the anomalous metric.
evaluate.fallback_message: Review the supply breakdown for each third-party supply line item above and record whether anomalous placements were found and whether they were addressed.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the Amazon DSP campaigns for {{site_name}} have third-party exchange supply line items where IVT filtering has not been confirmed and where supply-level performance metrics show anomalous patterns. Amazon's default filtering covers owned-and-operated inventory, but third-party supply requires active review of placement-level data to identify non-human activity. {{voice_recommend}} pulling a supply breakdown in the Amazon DSP console, excluding domains and apps with high impression volume but no downstream conversion activity, and confirming with the Amazon account team which IVT controls apply to the exchanges the account accesses.

audit_checks.title: Domains and apps with anomalous performance have not been excluded from delivery
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Placements showing click-through rates, view rates, or impression volumes that are outliers compared to the broader campaign are signals of low-quality or fraudulent traffic. Leaving them in delivery consumes budget and distorts the performance data used to evaluate the campaign.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP allows advertisers to exclude specific domains and apps at the line-item or order level. This exclusion capability is the primary hands-on tool for removing placements that IVT filtering does not catch or that pass IVT filters but still generate low-quality activity.

Made-for-advertising (MFA) sites are a common source of anomalous performance on open exchange supply. An MFA site is built primarily to generate ad revenue rather than serve a real audience. These sites pass standard bot filters because they use real browsers, but their audience engages with ads at rates that do not reflect genuine interest. Signals of MFA activity include click-through rates that are high across all advertisers on the site (not just the current campaign), session durations of a few seconds, and bounce rates near 100% on any traffic that does reach an off-Amazon destination.

Amazon DSP also surfaces brand safety and suitability segments through its verification partnerships, which include controls for known fraud-heavy supply. These segments are applied at the line-item level and represent a complement to manual domain and app exclusions.

A few signs point to placements that warrant review or exclusion:
  • A domain or app has a click-through rate three or more times higher than the campaign average across all supply
  • A domain or app delivers a large share of impressions but produces no detail page views, no off-Amazon pixel fires, and no engagement signals the campaign tracks
  • The same domain or app appears in supply reports across multiple campaigns but has never been reviewed
You may want to run a supply report monthly for active campaigns and maintain a rolling exclusion list based on what that review surfaces.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For endemic advertisers, detail page view rate and new-to-brand purchase rate are the most reliable signals for identifying low-quality supply. A placement that delivers impressions but produces no Amazon Standard Identification Number (ASIN) detail page views is a strong candidate for exclusion. A real audience exposed to a product ad will navigate to the product detail page at a meaningful rate.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For non-endemic advertisers, the Amazon Ad Tag pixel firing on the off-Amazon landing page is the signal that a real user reached the destination. Placements where Amazon DSP reports clicks but the pixel does not fire at a corresponding rate point to click fraud or browser-level interception, and those placements are candidates for exclusion.

learn_more.source_title: A beginner's guide to brand safety

investigate.intro: Pull a supply report in the Amazon DSP console and identify domains and apps that show anomalous click-through rates or a pattern of impressions with no downstream conversion activity. Review the current exclusion list for each line item.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: In the Amazon DSP console, open Reports and create a supply report for the campaign period you want to review. Segment by domain and app.
Sort by impressions and identify the top 20-30 placements by volume. For each, note the click-through rate, detail page view rate, and any off-Amazon conversion signal the campaign tracks.
Flag any placement where the click-through rate is more than three times the campaign average, or where impressions are high with no downstream conversion signal across the entire reporting period.
Navigate to the line item's brand safety settings and review the existing domain and app exclusion list. Confirm whether the flagged placements are already excluded or still active in delivery.
Add the flagged placements to the exclusion list and note the date and the metric that triggered the exclusion, so the decision is documented for future review.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner reviews the supply report for each active third-party supply line item and records whether anomalous placements were identified and whether they were added to the exclusion list. A line item with no anomalous placements, or with anomalous placements already excluded, passes. A line item with anomalous placements still in active delivery is recorded as a finding with the domain or app name and the metric that flagged it.
evaluate.fallback_message: Run a supply report for each active third-party supply line item above and record whether anomalous placements were found, reviewed, and excluded.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more Amazon DSP line items for {{site_name}} have domains or apps in active delivery that show anomalous performance signals consistent with low-quality or fraudulent traffic. These placements pass IVT filters but produce click-through rates or impression volumes that are outliers compared to the rest of the campaign's supply, with no corresponding downstream conversion activity. {{voice_recommend}} running a supply report segmented by domain and app, excluding the flagged placements at the line-item level, and reviewing supply performance on a monthly cadence for active campaigns.

audit_checks.title: Fraud exclusion segments from Amazon's verification partners are not applied to third-party supply line items
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Amazon DSP makes fraud and brand safety segments available through its verification partnerships, including segments that filter known fraud-heavy supply. A line item that does not apply these segments misses a layer of protection that requires no ongoing manual review, leaving spend exposed to supply sources that verification partners have already identified as high-risk.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP integrates brand safety and verification segments from third-party measurement providers. These segments are pre-built lists that the verification partner maintains, covering supply sources and placements the partner has identified as fraudulent, MFA-adjacent, or otherwise high-risk. Applying a segment to a line item tells Amazon DSP to exclude inventory that falls within the segment's criteria, without requiring the advertiser to maintain their own exclusion list for those categories.

The segments available depend on which verification partnerships the account has active. The configuration happens at the line-item level under brand safety settings. A line item that runs on third-party supply without any fraud-exclusion segment applied is relying entirely on Amazon's baseline filtering, which covers GIVT but does not cover the supply categories verification partners actively monitor for SIVT.

Applying a fraud exclusion segment does reduce available inventory, which can affect delivery pacing on a tightly targeted line item. You may want to confirm the segment is applied, that pacing has not been affected to a degree that requires adjusting the bid or budget, and that the segment covers the supply types the line item accesses.

A few signs point to this configuration being absent:
  • The line item's brand safety settings show no third-party verification segment applied
  • The account has never set up a verification partnership or the integration has expired
  • The supply report includes placements that verification partner dashboards flag as problematic, but those placements are still in delivery
You may want to confirm the verification partnership is active in the account and that at least a fraud-exclusion segment is applied to line items running on open exchange supply.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For endemic advertisers, fraud exclusion segments protect the attribution signals that feed Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC) analysis. Fraudulent impressions that pass GIVT filters can produce false positive engagement signals, which skews the path-to-purchase and audience overlap analyses that AMC supports.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For non-endemic advertisers, fraud exclusion segments are a practical substitute for the post-campaign supply review that the manual exclusion process requires. A segment maintained by a verification partner covers supply risk categories that a practitioner reviewing a supply report after the fact may not recognize.

learn_more.source_title: A beginner's guide to brand safety
IAB/ABC International Spiders and Bots List

investigate.intro: Review each third-party supply line item's brand safety settings in the Amazon DSP console to confirm whether a fraud or IVT exclusion segment from a verification partner is applied.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Open the Amazon DSP console and navigate to the line item you want to review.
Open the line item's settings and locate the brand safety section. Review which segments, if any, are applied under the third-party verification category.
Note whether a fraud or IVT exclusion segment is present. If no segment is applied, note the line item name and the supply type it accesses.
If no verification partnership is active in the account, contact your Amazon DSP account team to confirm which partners are available and how to enable the integration.

For each line item running on open exchange or third-party supply without a fraud exclusion segment, add the appropriate segment and confirm that delivery pacing is not materially affected after the change.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per third-party supply line item, whether a fraud or IVT exclusion segment from a verification partner is applied under brand safety settings. A line item with a fraud exclusion segment applied passes. A line item running on third-party supply with no fraud exclusion segment is recorded as a finding with the line item name.
evaluate.fallback_message: Review the brand safety settings for each third-party supply line item above and record whether a fraud or IVT exclusion segment is applied.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more Amazon DSP line items for {{site_name}} are running on third-party supply without a fraud or IVT exclusion segment from a verification partner applied. Amazon's baseline filtering covers general invalid traffic, but SIVT and supply categories that verification partners flag as high-risk require a segment to be applied at the line-item level. {{voice_recommend}} confirming the verification partnership is active in the account, applying a fraud exclusion segment to each affected line item under brand safety settings, and confirming that delivery pacing is not materially affected after the segment is added.
